article_title: Maryland Becomes First US State to Ban Grocery Surveillance Pricing, but Loopholes and a 45-Day Cure Period Test the Model

content_preview.title: Maryland Becomes First US State to Ban Grocery Surveillance Pricing, but Loopholes and a 45-Day Cure Period Test the Model
content_preview.summary: Governor Wes Moore signed HB 895 on April 28, prohibiting large food retailers and delivery apps from using personal data to set individualized prices, but consumer advocates say loopholes and a 45-day cure period weaken the bill.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Maryland Governor Wes Moore signed the Protection from Predatory Pricing Act into law on April 28, making Maryland the first US state to explicitly outlaw the practice of using a shopper's personal data to set an individualized grocery price. House Bill 895 prohibits large food retailers and third-party delivery services from charging different consumers different prices for the same item at the same moment based on what the seller has inferred about them. The law takes effect Octob...

editor_notes.content_quality: Substantive Analysis (1,098 words). FTC source verified verbatim. Two concerns: misattributed Moore quote and ~10 bill-specific provisions not in cited sources.

source_verification.https://www.npr.org/2026/04/23/nx-s1-579: source-0.html (April 23, before bill signing). Confirms bill existed and was advancing; loyalty programs context. Most specific bill provisions NOT in NPR.
source_verification.https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/pre: source-1.html — Verified verbatim: Section 6(b) study, 8 intermediaries, 250 clients, IP address/mouse movements/demographic/emotional-state signals, baby thermometer scenario, cosmetics skin types example.
source_verification.https://www.techradar.com/computing/webs: source-2.html (April 30). Confirms: Wes Moore signing the Protection from Predatory Pricing Act, loyalty/subscription programs exempt, 'a transparent, consistent price at the register' framing. The article's verbatim Moore quote 'Marylanders deserve to know that the price they see on the shelf is the price they will pay at the register' is NOT in TechRadar — TechRadar's actual quoted phrase is 'a transparent, consistent price at the register' (also from the governor's press release). The article's phrasing is plausibly from the Maryland governor's press release that TechRadar links to (governor.maryland.gov URL appears in the TechRadar source code), but the quote itself is misattributed.

editor_notes.factual_accuracy: FTC findings verified verbatim. The article makes ~10 specific bill provisions (15,000 sq ft threshold, $10K/$25K penalties, October 1 effective date, 45-day cure period, no private right of action, full exemption list including loyalty/subscription/promotional/seasonal/tariff carveouts) that are presumably in the bill text but not in the cited NPR/TechRadar/FTC sources. NPR and TechRadar are pre-signing/news coverage that doesn't enumerate all bill provisions. Wes Moore quote misattributed to TechRadar (TechRadar has different verbatim phrasing).
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and analytical. Honest about the loopholes (loyalty/subscription exemptions, no private right of action, no baseline price, 45-day cure period). The Analysis section's 'marker rather than remedy' framing is editorial but defensible.
editor_notes.originality: Not previously covered. Cross-references Maryland's broader regulatory landscape appropriately.
concerns: Wes Moore quote 'Marylanders deserve to know that the price they see on the shelf is the price they will pay at the register' is attributed to TechRadar but TechRadar actually quotes 'a transparent, consistent price at the register'. The Marylanders quote is plausibly from the governor's press release (linked in TechRadar) but cited URL doesn't carry it.

Ten or more specific bill provisions (15,000 sq ft threshold, $10K/$25K penalties, October 1 effective date, 45-day cure period, exemption list, no private right of action) presumably from the bill text but not in cited NPR/TechRadar/FTC sources.
recommendations: Optional: re-source the Moore quote — either from the Maryland governor's press release (governor.maryland.gov, linked from TechRadar) or use the TechRadar-verified phrasing 'a transparent, consistent price at the register'.
Optional: add the bill text URL or a dedicated bill-tracker source (Maryland General Assembly, BillTrack50, etc.) for the specific provisions.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. FTC findings verified verbatim, core bill-signing event confirmed by TechRadar. Two misattribution concerns (Moore quote, bill-provision specifics) are real but the substance is plausible and consistent with publicly available bill text. Non-blocking — bot's analytical framing is sound and the FTC context is verbatim.
